rows.title: Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate Reports 5% Rise in Data Breach Notifications in Q1 2026
rows.summary:
Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) has published its first-quarter 2026 breach notification statistics, finding that organisations submitted 68 data breach notifications between January and March 2026, representing a 5 per cent increase compared with the same period in 2025.

On 15 April 2026, the Data Protection Inspectorate published its Q1 2026 breach notification report, covering notifications received under the applicable data protection framework. The Inspectorate reports that no fines were reported in connection with this statistical release. AKI finds that 19 notifications were submitted in January, 23 in February, and 26 in March, indicating a steady increase across the quarter.

AKI finds that 31 of the 68 incidents involved the public sector, including state authorities, local governments, and entities or companies administered by them. The Inspectorate identifies several illustrative cases from the period. These include a medical record error in which a blood test was incorrectly registered under another patient's name, causing inaccurate data to appear in a health portal. AKI also identifies an e-commerce server compromise in which weakly protected infrastructure was used to install cryptomining software, with the attacker also gaining access to customer order data. A further case involved a misconfigured analytics service provider server that exposed client profiles, which were subsequently downloaded by a third party. Additional incidents noted by AKI include an account compromised via a phishing email granting access to file resources, a management system breach using credentials stolen from employee devices, and an SMS phishing attack used to obtain authentication data from employees.

AKI identifies information-stealing malware, citing examples such as RedLine and Vidar, as one of the more concealed threats on the current cyber threat landscape. The Inspectorate finds that such malware is frequently downloaded to personal devices via unlicensed software, malicious advertisements, or fraudulent links generated with artificial intelligence assistance. AKI further finds that these tools steal not only passwords but also session cookies, enabling attackers to bypass multi-factor authentication by hijacking active sessions. The Inspectorate also identifies risks associated with AI-assisted code generation, known as vibe coding, noting that several national cybersecurity authorities have found that while such tools accelerate development, they may introduce security vulnerabilities that developers without software expertise are unlikely to detect, and that insecure generated code can permit unauthorised data access or expose sensitive information.

rows.title: EDPB Annual Report 2025: 414 Cross-Border Cases Initiated and EUR 1.15 Billion in National Fines Recorded
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board published its 2025 annual report on 9 April 2026, setting out the body's activities over the preceding year, including cross-border enforcement data, new guidelines, adequacy opinions, and international cooperation work conducted under its 2024–2027 strategy.

On 9 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board released its annual report covering 2025, as reported by the Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate. According to the EDPB report, data protection authorities across EU member states imposed a combined total of EUR 1.15 billion in fines at national level during the year. The EDPB also recorded 414 cross-border cases initiated and 1,299 one-stop-shop procedures under Art. 60 GDPR, of which 572 resulted in a final decision.

The EDPB states that the body adopted three new sets of guidelines in 2025, addressing pseudonymisation, blockchain technologies, and the intersection of the Digital Services Act with the GDPR. According to the report, the EDPB and the European Data Protection Supervisor issued a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal to amend the EU legal framework, including certain provisions of the GDPR. The EDPB additionally adopted five adequacy-related opinions concerning the United Kingdom, Brazil, and the European Patent Organisation, alongside recommendations on the legal basis for mandatory user account creation on e-commerce platforms and recommendations relating to the World Anti-Doping Agency's 2027 World Anti-Doping Code.

According to the report, a central development of the year was the adoption of the Helsinki Declaration, which the EDPB states is directed at increasing clarity, support, and engagement to make GDPR compliance more accessible and to strengthen cooperation among stakeholders. EDPB Chair Anu Talus is quoted in the report as stating that the rapidly expanding EU digital regulatory framework has made the data protection ecosystem more complex, and that the EDPB focused on increasing legal certainty and making compliance more achievable in practice. The report also documents the EDPB's continued international engagement, including participation in the G7 Data Protection Authorities Roundtable, the Global Privacy Assembly, and a second online meeting in December 2025 with commissioners and supervisory authority representatives from adequacy decision countries and organisations.

rows.title: Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate Reports Record Complaints and Apotheka Fine in 2025 Annual Review
rows.summary:
Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) has published its 2025 annual report, recording a record number of complaints received during the year and highlighting the Apotheka customer data breach as the period's most prominent enforcement case, resolved out of court with a record fine under the GDPR.

On 31 March 2026, AKI published its annual report for 2025, detailing supervisory activity, enforcement outcomes and institutional priorities across the preceding calendar year. The report does not specify the exact fine amount in the source text, though AKI characterises the out-of-court settlement in the Apotheka matter as a record-level financial penalty. No EUR equivalent is provided in the source material.

AKI finds that the record volume of complaints received in 2025 reflects increased public awareness and growing expectations that the state respond to data protection violations more swiftly and consistently. Director General Pille Lehis notes in the report's foreword that the Inspectorate operates in an increasingly complex legal environment, in which new European Union digital regulations are bringing additional roles and expanded responsibilities to the authority. AKI states that the Apotheka case, concerning a leak of customer personal data, concluded in 2025 and exposed serious deficiencies in the implementation of security measures, engaging obligations under Art. 32 GDPR.

AKI identifies key supervisory themes for the year, including employee monitoring, the legal framework for sobriety testing, and the document registers of local authorities, where AKI finds that the public disclosure of sensitive personal data continues to occur. The Inspectorate also notes an increased need for preventive work, having organised regional training sessions, developed data protection networks, and directed particular attention to the education sector and the use of artificial intelligence in schools. AKI states that data protection is not solely a technical requirement but a matter of trust and transparency, and the report sets out a risk-based approach and strengthened preventive activity as the focus of the Inspectorate's new strategic period.

rows.title: Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate Opens Own-Initiative Proceedings Over Website Publishing Personal Data Linked to Court Decisions
rows.summary:
Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) has opened own-initiative supervisory proceedings concerning the website ettevaatust.ee, which publishes personal data alongside references to court judgments, acting under its supervisory powers pursuant to the General Data Protection Regulation.

On 27 March 2026, the Andmekaitse Inspektsioon announced that it is aware of the website ettevaatust.ee and has initiated proceedings on its own motion. No fine has been issued at this stage.

AKI states that all possible further steps will be decided collectively in respect of the website as a whole. Accordingly, AKI finds that it will not open separate supervisory proceedings in response to each individual complaint submitted about the site. The authority notes, however, that this approach does not remove an individual's right to submit an objection or other request directly to the operator of the website, nor does it limit the right to bring proceedings directly before a court.

AKI states that individuals whose personal data have been published on the site are required, in the first instance, to contact the data controller, namely the website operator, with any questions, objections or requests relating to the disclosure of their data (Art. 77 GDPR). The authority finds that it is the data controller that bears the obligation and responsibility to explain the legal basis on which data are processed, what data protection conditions apply, and how the rights of data subjects are safeguarded, including the rights to rectification and erasure (Arts. 16 and 17 GDPR). AKI states that the data controller is required to respond to any such request without undue delay and in any event within one month (Art. 12(3) GDPR).

AKI directs individuals seeking further information about their rights to resources published on the authority's own website, covering access to personal data, rectification, erasure, and the submission of objections.
